originalEn.event: Born Constantine Psellos in Constantinople.
Begins his political career at the court of Emperor Michael V.
Appointed 'Consul of the Philosophers' (Hypatos ton Philosophon), effectively head of the University of Constantinople.
Under pressure, he enters a monastery and takes the monastic name Michael.
Recalled to the imperial court by Empress Theodora.
Composes the 'Chronographia,' his most famous historical work.
Believed to have died around this year, though the exact date is uncertain.
Born in the village of Mishaninskaya (later renamed Lomonosovo) in Arkhangelsk Governorate, Russia.
At age 19, leaves his village and walks to Moscow to enroll in the Slavic Greek Latin Academy.
Is sent to Germany to study chemistry, physics, and metallurgy at the University of Marburg.
Returns to Russia and is appointed as an adjunct professor at the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences.
Establishes the first research and teaching chemical laboratory in Russia.
Co-founds Moscow State University with his patron, Ivan Shuvalov, based on his proposed plan.
Observes the transit of Venus and is the first to discover and document the presence of an atmosphere on the planet.
Dies of pneumonia in St. Petersburg and is buried in the Lazarevskoe Cemetery.
Born in Hadiach, Poltava Governorate, Russian Empire.
Studied at Kyiv University and became a prominent member of the 'Hromada' student society.
Became a docent (lecturer) in ancient history at Kyiv University.
Dismissed from his post following the Ems Ukaz, which banned the Ukrainian language, and went into political exile.
Founded the influential Ukrainian-language political journal 'Hromada' in Geneva, Switzerland.
Accepted a professorship at the University of Sofia in Bulgaria.
Died in Sofia, Bulgaria, and was buried there.
Born in Chełm, Congress Poland, Russian Empire.
Appointed Professor of History at Lviv University in Austria-Hungary.
Publishes the first volume of his magnum opus, 'History of Ukraine-Rus''.
Elected head of the Central Rada (Council) in Kyiv following the February Revolution.
Elected President of the Ukrainian People's Republic.
Lives in exile in Vienna and Prague after the collapse of the republic.
Returns to Soviet Ukraine to continue his academic work.
Arrested by the Soviet secret police (OGPU) and exiled to Moscow.
Dies under mysterious circumstances in Kislovodsk, Russia.
페르가나 계곡의 안디잔에서 출생.
12세의 나이로 페르가나의 왕위에 오름.
처음으로 사마르칸트를 점령했으나 곧 잃음.
카불을 점령하고 새로운 거점으로 삼음.
제1차 파니파트 전투에서 이브라힘 로디를 격파하고 델리를 점령.
칸와 전투에서 라지푸트 연합군을 격파하고 지배권을 확고히 함.
무굴 제국의 아그라에서 사망.
Born as a 'porphyrogennitos' (born in the purple), a son of Emperor Romanos II and Empress Theophano.
Becomes senior emperor after the death of John I Tzimiskes, but real power is held by the eunuch Basil Lekapenos.
Faces and eventually crushes a series of large-scale internal rebellions led by the powerful generals Bardas Skleros and Bardas Phokas.
Forms a crucial alliance with Vladimir I of Kiev, receiving 6,000 Norse mercenaries (the foundation of the Varangian Guard) in exchange for his sister Anna's hand in marriage.
Achieves a decisive victory over the Bulgarian army at the Battle of Kleidion.
Completes the conquest of the First Bulgarian Empire, annexing it fully into the Byzantine Empire.
Dies while planning a military expedition to reclaim Sicily, leaving the empire at its territorial and financial zenith.
Born in Pingyang, which is modern-day Linfen, Shanxi, China.
Departs from the Eastern Jin capital of Chang'an to travel to India in search of Buddhist scriptures.
Successfully crosses the Taklamakan Desert and the treacherous Pamir Mountains, arriving in Northwest India.
Travels extensively through northern India, studying Sanskrit and collecting scriptures, spending three years in Pataliputra.
Travels to Sri Lanka (the 'Lion Kingdom') to obtain more texts, then begins his perilous sea voyage home.
After surviving severe storms and an unplanned stop in Java, he finally arrives back in China at Laoshan, in modern Shandong.
Resides in Jiankang (modern Nanjing), where he translates the scriptures he collected and dictates his travelogue, 'A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms' (Foguo Ji).
Dies at Xin Monastery in Jingzhou at the approximate age of 85.
Born in Germania, a fortified town in what is now modern Bulgaria.
Appointed as a general on the eastern frontier by Emperor Justin I.
Plays a key role in suppressing the Nika riots in Constantinople, saving Emperor Justinian's throne.
Leads the Vandalic War, conquering the Vandal Kingdom in North Africa in less than a year.
Leads the initial and most significant campaigns of the Gothic War to reconquer Italy from the Ostrogoths.
Captures the Ostrogothic capital of Ravenna and their king, Witiges.
In retirement, he is recalled to defend Constantinople from a Kutrigur Bulgar invasion, which he successfully repels.
Accused of conspiracy against Justinian, he is disgraced and briefly imprisoned before being pardoned.
Dies in Constantinople, a few months after his Emperor, Justinian.
Born into a family of minor Ukrainian nobility.
Captured by the Ottomans at the Battle of Cecora and spent two years in captivity.
The "Chyhyryn affair" where Polish official Daniel Czapliński seizes his estate, leading to a personal vendetta that sparks a national rebellion.
Begins the Khmelnytsky Uprising, securing major victories at Zhovti Vody and Korsun.
Signs the Treaty of Zboriv, which recognizes the Cossack Hetmanate as an autonomous entity within the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Suffers a major defeat at the Battle of Berestechko after being abandoned by his Tatar allies.
Signs the Treaty of Pereiaslav, placing the Cossack Hetmanate under the protection of the Tsar of Muscovy.
Dies in Chyhyryn, leaving behind a nascent but unstable state.
Emerged as chieftain of the Ashina Turks, vassals of the Rouran Khaganate.
Suppressed a Tiele revolt for the Rouran and requested a Rouran princess in marriage as a reward.
The Rouran Khagan Anagui refused and insulted Bumin, sparking the Turkic rebellion.
Allied with the Western Wei, married Princess Changle, and defeated the Rouran forces. Rouran Khagan Anagui committed suicide.
Declared himself Ilig Qaghan at the Otuken capital, founding the First Turkic Khaganate.
Died shortly after founding the Khaganate. His brother Istämi was appointed Yabgu (ruler) of the western territories.
Born, the illegitimate son of Sviatoslav I of Kyiv.
Defeats and kills his half-brother Yaropolk, becoming Grand Prince of Kyiv.
Is baptized into Orthodox Christianity in Chersonesus, marries Byzantine princess Anna Porphyrogenita.
Organizes the mass baptism of the people of Kyiv in the Dnieper River, initiating the Christianization of Rus'.
Dies at Berestove, near Kyiv, after ruling for 35 years.
Born in Saint Petersburg, Russian Empire.
Graduates from Saint Petersburg State University.
Becomes a full member of the St. Petersburg Academy of Sciences.
Founds and becomes the first president of the Ukrainian Academy of Sciences in Kyiv.
Becomes director of the newly founded Radium Institute in Petrograd.
Publishes his seminal work, "The Biosphere," in Russian (French translation in 1929).
Publishes his final paper on the noosphere.
Dies in Moscow, Soviet Union.
Born in Zhytomyr, Russian Empire (now Ukraine).
Co-founds the Group for the Study of Reactive Motion (GIRD), a key Soviet rocketry research group.
Arrested during Stalin's Great Purge and sentenced to 10 years in a labor camp.
Released from prison by a special decree, though his conviction was not cleared until 1957.
As Chief Designer, he oversees the successful launch of Sputnik 1, the world's first artificial satellite.
Oversees the Vostok 1 mission, which makes Yuri Gagarin the first human to orbit the Earth.
Dies during surgery. His identity as the 'Chief Designer' is finally revealed to the public, and he is buried with state honors in the Kremlin Wall Necropolis.
Born in Thessaly, Byzantine Empire.
Moved to Constantinople to teach privately after studying on the island of Andros.
Appointed Archbishop of Thessalonica by Emperor Theophilos.
Deposed as Archbishop following the official end of Byzantine Iconoclasm.
His fame reaches the Abbasid Caliph, who invites him to Baghdad. Leo refuses, gaining favor with the Byzantine Emperor.
Appointed head (scholarch) of the newly re-founded Magnaura School by the regent Bardas.
Last known historical mention of him. His exact death date is unknown.
Born as Ibrahim Qunanbaiuly in the Chingis Mountains, modern-day Kazakhstan.
Attended a madrasa in Semipalatinsk, followed by a Russian school, giving him a unique dual education.
Began to write poetry with serious dedication, adopting the pen name 'Abai'.
Wrote his major philosophical work, the 'Book of Words' (Kara Sozder).
Suffered the death of his son, Abdirakhman.
Died in the Zhidebay valley, shortly after the death of his other son, Magauiya.
Born in the village of Grigorovo, Nizhny Novgorod region, into a priestly family.
Begins his public opposition to the liturgical reforms initiated by Patriarch Nikon of Moscow.
Arrested and exiled to Siberia with his family. Endures a decade of extreme hardship and travel.
Briefly returns to Moscow but refuses to compromise, leading to his re-arrest and exile to the Russian North.
Defrocked and anathematized by the Great Moscow Synod. Imprisoned in an underground pit in Pustozersk.
Writes his autobiography, 'The Life of Avvakum, Written by Himself', a foundational work of Russian literature.
Burned at the stake in Pustozersk along with his closest followers for 'great blasphemies against the Tsar'.
Becomes co-ruler of the Huns with his brother Bleda.
Murders Bleda to become the sole ruler of the Hunnic Empire.
Launches a major invasion of the Balkans and threatens Constantinople.
Invades Gaul and fights the Battle of the Catalaunian Plains.
Invades Italy, sacks several cities, but spares Rome.
Dies on his wedding night; his empire collapses shortly after.
Born in Sayram, now in Kazakhstan.
Travels to Bukhara to study under the renowned Sufi master Yusuf Hamadani.
Briefly becomes the head of Hamadani's khaneqah (Sufi lodge) before returning to his hometown of Yasi.
At age 63, in mourning for the Prophet Muhammad, he retreats to an underground cell (chilla-khana) to live out his life in contemplation.
Dies in the city of Yasi (present-day Turkistan, Kazakhstan), where his mausoleum now stands.
Born in the Porphyra chamber of the Great Palace of Constantinople, as the first child of Emperor Alexios I Komnenos and Irene Doukaina.
As a teenager, she witnesses the arrival of the leaders of the First Crusade in Constantinople, an event she would later chronicle in detail.

Her father, Alexios I, dies. She and her mother attempt to persuade him to name her husband, Nikephoros Bryennios, as his successor, but fail. She then conspires against her brother, the new Emperor John II.
After her plot is discovered, she is stripped of her property and exiled to the Kecharitomene Monastery, which her mother had founded.
Following the death of her husband, she begins writing the Alexiad, a 15-book history of her father's reign, likely completing a work her husband had started.
Dies in the monastery, having completed her monumental historical work.
Born, likely in the Principality of Moscow, during the era of the Tatar Yoke.
Collaborated with Theophanes the Greek and Prokhor of Gorodets on the frescoes for the Cathedral of the Annunciation in the Moscow Kremlin.
Painted frescoes and the iconostasis in the Dormition Cathedral in Vladimir, alongside his friend and collaborator Daniil Chyorny.
Painted his most famous work, the icon of 'The Trinity', for the iconostasis of the Trinity Cathedral at the Trinity Lavra of St. Sergius.
Died at the Andronikov Monastery in Moscow, where he had painted his last works in the Saviour Cathedral.
Canonized as a saint by the Russian Orthodox Church, with his feast day on July 4th.
Born in Moscow.
Attends the Imperial Lyceum at Tsarskoye Selo.
Publishes his first long poem, 'Ruslan and Ludmila'; exiled for political verses.
Writes the historical drama 'Boris Godunov'.
Marries Natalya Goncharova; completes 'Eugene Onegin'.
Fatally wounded in a duel with Georges d'Anthès and dies two days later.
Born in Constantinople.
Ascends to the throne after a successful coup, beginning his reign as Byzantine Emperor.
Issues a Golden Bull granting extensive trading privileges to the Venetians in exchange for naval support against the Normans.
Decisively defeats the Pechenegs at the Battle of Levounion, ending their threat to Constantinople.
Sends envoys to Pope Urban II at the Council of Piacenza, requesting military aid against the Seljuk Turks.
The armies of the First Crusade arrive at Constantinople; Alexios secures oaths of fealty from their leaders.
Byzantine forces, in cooperation with the Crusaders, recapture Nicaea, Smyrna, Ephesus, and other key Anatolian territories.
Dies in Constantinople, leaving a stabilized and recovering empire to his son, John II Komnenos.
Born in Herat, then a major city in the Timurid Empire (present-day Afghanistan).
Forced to flee Herat with his family after the death of the Timurid ruler Shahrukh.
Returns to Herat after his childhood friend, Husayn Bayqara, becomes the ruler. He is appointed as a high-ranking official and advisor.
Appointed as vizier (chief minister), gaining significant political power and influence.
Composes his magnum opus, the 'Khamsa' (Quintet), a collection of five epic poems in the Chagatai language.
Completes 'Muhakamat al-Lughatayn' (The Comparison of the Two Languages), a treatise arguing for the literary superiority of Turkic over Persian.
Dies in Herat, leaving behind a vast legacy of literary works and architectural patronage.
Born in Bukhara, Transoxiana (present-day Uzbekistan).
Reportedly lost his sight in early childhood but miraculously regained it.
At age 16, he traveled to Mecca for the Hajj pilgrimage and began his extensive journeys to collect hadith.
Spent over 16 years traveling throughout the Abbasid Caliphate, including major centers of learning in Iraq, Syria, Egypt, and the Hejaz.
Completed his magnum opus, 'Sahih al-Bukhari', after meticulously sifting through over 600,000 narrations.
Faced a theological dispute in Nishapur, leading to his exile.
Died in Khartank, a village near Samarkand, after being exiled from his hometown of Bukhara.
Born in the Fergana Valley, Transoxiana (modern-day Uzbekistan).
Moved to Baghdad and began working at the House of Wisdom under the patronage of Caliph al-Ma'mun.
Wrote his most famous work, 'Elements of Astronomy' (Jawāmiʿ ʿIlm al-Nujūm).
Participated in the project to measure the diameter of the Earth in the Syrian desert.
Traveled to Egypt to design and supervise the construction of the Great Nilometer on Rawda Island, Fustat (Old Cairo).
His exact death year is unknown, but his last recorded activity was in 861.
블라디미르 대공의 아들로 태어남.
블라디미르 대공 사망 후, 형제간의 왕위 계승 전쟁 시작.
알타 강 전투에서 형 스뱌토폴크를 물리치고 키예프 대공이 됨.
최초의 루스 법전인 '루스카야 프라브다'의 초기 버전을 제정함.
키예프 공성전에서 페체네그족을 결정적으로 격파하고 루스의 유일한 통치자가 됨.
승리를 기념하여 키예프에 성 소피아 대성당 건설을 시작함.
다섯 아들에게 영토를 나누어준 후 비슈호로드에서 사망함.
Born, likely in a village on the Northern Dvina River. His early life is largely undocumented.
Gained notoriety as a Cossack ataman, possibly engaging in piracy and mercenary activities along the Volga and Don rivers.
Hired by the Stroganov merchant family, Yermak leads a force of over 800 Cossacks across the Ural Mountains to confront the Khanate of Sibir.
Achieves a decisive victory against Khan Kuchum's forces at the Battle of Chuvash Cape.
Captures Qashliq (Isker), the capital of the Khanate of Sibir, forcing Khan Kuchum to flee into the steppes.
Yermak sends his lieutenant Ivan Koltso to Moscow with news of the conquest and a tribute of furs. Tsar Ivan the Terrible officially pardons the Cossacks and sends reinforcements.
Ambushed by Khan Kuchum's forces while camping on an island in the Irtysh River. Yermak drowns, allegedly weighed down by the chainmail armor gifted to him by the Tsar.
Born in Antioch to a noble family.
Begins an ascetic life as a hermit in the mountains near Antioch after his mother's death.
Ordained a presbyter (priest) in Antioch and begins his celebrated preaching career.
Consecrated Archbishop of Constantinople against his will, becoming a major figure in the imperial capital.
Condemned and briefly exiled by the Synod of the Oak, orchestrated by his political and ecclesiastical enemies.
Exiled a second time by Empress Eudoxia, this time to Cucusus in Armenia.
Dies during a forced march to a more remote place of exile in Pityus on the Black Sea.
Born Mīrzā Muhammad Tāraghay bin Shāhrukh in Soltaniyeh, Persia.
Appointed as the governor of Samarkand at the age of 16 by his father, Shah Rukh.
Begins construction of the Ulugh Beg Madrasa (an Islamic school) on the Registan square in Samarkand.
Builds the great Ulugh Beg Observatory in Samarkand to advance the study of astronomy.
Completes the 'Zij-i Sultani' (Sultanic Star Tables), a monumental star catalogue and astronomical treatise.
Ascends to the throne of the Timurid Empire after his father Shah Rukh's death, leading to a period of instability.
Defeated in battle by his rebellious son, Abd al-Latif Mirza, and subsequently assassinated near Samarkand.
Birth of Flavius Petrus Sabbatius in Tauresium.
Ascends to the throne as Emperor Justinian I.
The Nika riots erupt in Constantinople and are brutally suppressed.
The Corpus Juris Civilis (Body of Civil Law) is compiled and published.
General Belisarius reconquers North Africa from the Vandals.
The rebuilt Hagia Sophia is consecrated in Constantinople.
The Plague of Justinian begins, a pandemic that devastates the empire's population.
The Gothic War concludes, with Byzantium nominally controlling Italy after a long and destructive conflict.
Death of Justinian I in Constantinople.
Born in Moscow to Grand Prince Vasily II.
Ascends to the throne as Grand Prince of Moscow.
Defeats the army of the Novgorod Republic at the Battle of Shelon River.
Formally annexes the Republic of Novgorod, ending its independence.
The Great Stand on the Ugra River, which marks the de facto end of the Mongol-Tatar yoke over Russia.
Annexes the Principality of Tver, his last major Russian rival.
Issues the Sudebnik, a new code of laws for the unified state.
Dies in Moscow, leaving a vastly expanded and centralized state to his son, Vasily III.
Becomes Grand Prince of Moscow at age three.
Crowned as the first 'Tsar of All Russia'.
Conquers the Khanate of Kazan.
Conquers the Khanate of Astrakhan.
His wife, Anastasia Romanovna, dies, marking a turn in his personality.
Establishes the Oprichnina, a period of mass repression.
Leads the Massacre of Novgorod.
Kills his eldest son and heir, Ivan Ivanovich, in a fit of rage.
Dies, leaving the throne to his younger, unfit son Feodor.
Born in Mazepyntsi, near Bila Tserkva, then part of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.
Elected Hetman of the Zaporizhian Host of Left-bank Ukraine.
Ruled as Hetman, initially as a loyal vassal to Tsar Peter the Great of Russia, and became a great patron of arts and culture.
Briefly united both Left-bank and Right-bank Ukraine under his rule during an uprising against Poland.
Secretly allied with Charles XII of Sweden against Peter the Great during the Great Northern War.
Suffered a decisive defeat alongside the Swedes at the Battle of Poltava.
Died in exile in Bendery (Tighina), then part of the Ottoman Empire.
Born in Nahuievychi, Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria (modern-day Ukraine).
Enrolls at Lviv University and begins publishing his first literary works.
First arrested for socialist activities and belonging to a secret society.
Co-founds the Ruthenian-Ukrainian Radical Party, the first modern Ukrainian political party.
Defends his doctoral dissertation, 'Barlaam and Josaphat', at the University of Vienna.
Publishes his philosophical epic poem 'Moses' (Мойсей), a cornerstone of his literary legacy.
Nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
Dies in Lviv at the age of 59, impoverished but revered as a national icon.
Born in Avcıköy, Crimea, Russian Empire.
Travels to Paris, where he is influenced by French liberal thought and socialist ideas.
First attempts to get permission from the Russian government to publish a newspaper in a Turkic language, but is denied.
Successfully founds the newspaper Terciman (The Interpreter) in Bakhchysarai, which becomes a voice for Turkic intellectuals.
Establishes a 'New Method' (Usul-i Jadid) school in Bakhchysarai, pioneering his educational reforms.
Elected chairman of the First All-Russian Muslim Congress, a major step in organizing the political activities of Muslims in the empire.
Dies in Bakhchysarai, Crimea, leaving a legacy of reform and enlightenment.
Born in Kashan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Observed a lunar eclipse in Kashan, which he later used for his calculations.
Completed his work 'Sullam al-Sama' ('The Stairway of Heaven') on the sizes and distances of celestial bodies.
Was invited by Prince Ulugh Beg to join the scientific center in Samarkand.
Completed 'The Treatise on the Circumference,' calculating 2π to 9 sexagesimal digits, equivalent to 16 decimal places.
Completed his magnum opus, 'Miftah al-Hisab' ('The Key to Arithmetic'), which explains the use of decimal fractions.
Died in Samarkand, under circumstances that are still debated by historians.
Born into a wealthy family in Constantinople.
Participated in the bride-show for Emperor Theophilos, where her famous witty exchange took place.
Founded a convent in Constantinople and became its first abbess, following the end of the second Iconoclast period.
Died. The exact year of her death is unknown.
Venerated as a saint in the Eastern Orthodox Church. Her feast day is celebrated on September 7th.
Born in Constantinople to Emperor Manuel II Palaiologos and Helena Dragaš.
Becomes co-Despot of the Morea, a Byzantine province in the Peloponnese, with his brothers.
Ascends to the Byzantine throne as Constantine XI Palaiologos following the death of his brother, John VIII.
Mehmed II becomes the Ottoman Sultan and begins preparations for the conquest of Constantinople.
The final siege of Constantinople by the Ottoman Empire begins.
Dies in battle during the final assault as the city falls to the Ottomans, marking the end of the Byzantine Empire.
Born as the grandson of Genghis Khan.
Proclaimed Great Khan of the Mongol Empire after defeating his brother Ariq Böke.
Officially established the Yuan dynasty and proclaimed himself Emperor of China.
Marco Polo allegedly arrives at Kublai Khan's court in Dadu (Beijing).
Completed the conquest of the Southern Song dynasty, unifying all of China under his rule.
Launched two naval invasions of Japan, both of which failed, partly due to major storms.
Died in Dadu at the age of 78.
Born in Kyiv, daughter of Grand Prince Yaroslav the Wise of Kievan Rus'.
Marries King Henry I of France in Reims Cathedral, becoming Queen of the Franks.
Gives birth to her first son and future king, Philip I.
King Henry I dies. Anne serves as regent for the eight-year-old Philip I.
Marries Count Ralph IV of Valois, causing a scandal and her temporary excommunication.
Last recorded signature on a document under her son Philip's reign. Her exact death date is unknown.
Born in Pskov (approximate date) and later married Igor of Kyiv.
Igor of Kyiv is murdered by the Drevlians. Olga becomes regent for her young son, Sviatoslav.
Carries out a four-part, systematic revenge against the Drevlians, culminating in the burning of their capital, Iskorosten.
Institutes administrative reforms, establishing a network of 'pogosti' (administrative centers) for systematic tax collection.
Travels to Constantinople and is baptized into Orthodox Christianity, taking the Christian name Elena (Helen).
Dies in Kyiv. She was later canonized as a saint by the Orthodox Church.
Born into serfdom in the village of Moryntsi, Russian Empire.
Freed from serfdom through the efforts of Karl Bryullov and Vasily Zhukovsky.
Publishes his first and most famous collection of poetry, 'Kobzar'.
Arrested for his involvement in the political Brotherhood of Saints Cyril and Methodius and exiled.
Released from his ten-year military exile after the death of Tsar Nicholas I.
Dies in Saint Petersburg at the age of 47. He was later reburied in Ukraine.
Tamar is born to King George III and Queen Burdukhan.
Appointed co-ruler and heir apparent by her father, George III.
Becomes the sole monarch of Georgia upon her father's death, taking the title 'Mepe' (King).
Is forced by the nobility to marry the Rus' prince Yuri Bogolyubsky.
Divorces and banishes Yuri due to his misconduct.
Chooses and marries the Alan prince David Soslan.
Georgian armies led by David Soslan achieve a major victory at the Battle of Shamkor.
Georgian forces win a decisive victory over the Seljuk Sultanate of Rûm at the Battle of Basian.
Helps establish the Empire of Trebizond on the Black Sea coast as a Georgian client state.
Tamar dies, leaving behind a powerful and prosperous kingdom. She is later canonized as a saint.
Born in Constantinople or Cyprus, to a family of low social standing; her father was a bear keeper for the Hippodrome.
Meets Justinian, the heir to the throne, in Constantinople. She had been working as an actress and courtesan.
Marries Justinian. He had the law changed to permit their marriage, as it previously forbade officials from marrying actresses.
Crowned Augusta (Empress) alongside Justinian as Augustus (Emperor) of the Byzantine Empire.
Plays a pivotal role in convincing Justinian to stay and crush the Nika Riots, effectively saving his throne.
Enacts significant social reforms, including laws that prohibited forced prostitution and expanded women's rights in divorce and property ownership.
Dies, likely from cancer, in Constantinople. Justinian mourned her deeply and never remarried.
Born in the Byzantine Empire.
Arrives in the Russian lands, first documented in Novgorod.
Paints the frescoes in the Church of the Transfiguration on Ilin Street, Novgorod.
Moves to Moscow and works on various commissions, including for the Grand Prince.
Works on the iconostasis of the Cathedral of the Annunciation in the Moscow Kremlin with Andrei Rublev and Prokhor of Gorodets.
Dies, likely in Moscow.
Born into the Ashina clan and later educated in the Tang capital of Chang'an.
Escapes from Tang control and persuades Qutlugh (later Ilterish Khagan) to revolt against the Tang Dynasty.
The Second Turkic Khaganate is founded; Tonyukuk is appointed as the chief strategist and commander (Apa Tarkan).
Ilterish Khagan dies. Tonyukuk continues to serve as the chief advisor under the new Khagan, Qapaghan.
After Qapaghan Khagan is killed, Tonyukuk plays a crucial role in supporting Bilge Khagan to secure the throne against a coup.
Composes his own memorial inscriptions, one of the oldest surviving examples of Turkic literature, written in the first person.
Dies at an advanced age, having served three Khagans and secured the foundation of the empire.
Born near Kesh (modern Shahrisabz, Uzbekistan) into the Barlas tribe.
Sustains severe injuries to his right leg and arm, earning him the nickname 'Timur the Lame'.
Becomes the sovereign ruler of the Chagatai Khanate's western territories and establishes Samarkand as his capital.
Invades India and sacks the city of Delhi, causing immense destruction.
Conquers Baghdad and Damascus, expanding his empire westward.
Defeats and captures the Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara.
Dies in Otrar while on a campaign to conquer Ming China.
Born in Suceava, Moldavia, to a noble Romanian family (Movilă).
Becomes a monk at the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra (Monastery of the Caves).
Elected Archimandrite (head abbot) of the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra.
Founds a school at the Lavra, emphasizing both theology and liberal arts.
Elected Metropolitan of Kyiv, Halych, and all Rus'. He merges his school with the Kyiv Brotherhood School to form the Kyiv-Mohyla Collegium (later Academy).
Convenes a council in Kyiv to discuss and approve his catechism, which would become the influential 'Orthodox Confession of Faith'.
Dies in Kyiv, leaving his fortune to the Collegium and cementing his legacy as a major reformer and educator.
Born in Votkinsk, Russian Empire.
Studies at the newly founded Saint Petersburg Conservatory.
His Piano Concerto No. 1 premieres in Boston, becoming an instant success.
Premieres 'Swan Lake' to initial failure, enters a disastrous marriage, and begins his 13-year correspondence with his patron Nadezhda von Meck.
Composes his Fourth Symphony and the opera 'Eugene Onegin'.
The ballet 'The Sleeping Beauty' premieres. Nadezhda von Meck abruptly ends their relationship and patronage.
The ballet 'The Nutcracker' premieres in Saint Petersburg.
Conducts the premiere of his Symphony No. 6, the 'Pathétique', and dies nine days later.
Born in Caesarea, Palaestina Prima.
Appointed as adsessor (legal advisor) to General Belisarius.
Accompanies Belisarius on the campaign against the Vandals in North Africa.
Participates in the Gothic War in Italy.
Witnesses the outbreak of the Plague of Justinian in Constantinople.
Completes his major work, The Wars of Justinian.
Likely writes the Secret History (Anecdota).
Writes On the Buildings of Justinian.
Dies, likely in Constantinople.
Born in Cappadocia, son of the Exarch of Africa.
Overthrows the tyrannical Emperor Phocas and is proclaimed Emperor in Constantinople.
The Sassanid Persian army under Khosrow II captures Jerusalem and seizes the True Cross.
Personally leads a series of brilliant and daring military campaigns against the Sassanid Empire.
After defeating the Persians, recovers the True Cross and returns it to Jerusalem in a triumphal procession.
Makes Greek the official language of the Empire, replacing Latin, and adopts the title 'Basileus'.
The Byzantine army suffers a catastrophic defeat against the forces of the Rashidun Caliphate at the Battle of Yarmouk.
The provinces of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt are conquered by the advancing Arab armies.
Dies in Constantinople, having saved the empire from one foe only to see it dismembered by another.
Born Chen Yi near Luoyang, Henan.
Fully ordained as a Buddhist monk.
Begins his journey to India, defying the Emperor's ban on travel.
Travels throughout India, studying at Nalanda University and collecting scriptures.
Returns to the Tang capital, Chang'an, with 657 Buddhist texts.
Completes his famous travelogue, 'Great Tang Records on the Western Regions'.
Leads a large state-sponsored translation project until his death.
Dies in Tongchuan, Shaanxi.
Born in Chornukhy, Cossack Hetmanate, into a Cossack family.
Intermittently studied at the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, excelling in languages, music, and philosophy.
Served as a singer in the Imperial Chapel in St. Petersburg.
Traveled through Europe as part of a mission, likely visiting Hungary, Vienna, and other centers of learning.
Taught poetics and ethics at Pereiaslav and Kharkiv Collegiums, but was dismissed due to his unorthodox methods and ideas.
Began his life as an itinerant philosopher, wandering through Ukraine, teaching, and writing.
Died in the village of Ivanivka (now Skovorodynivka), after reportedly digging his own grave the day before his death.
Born in Washington, County Durham, England.
Graduates from Oxford with a first-class honours degree in modern history.
Begins serious study of Arabic and makes her first major journey into the Syrian desert.
Recruited by British Intelligence at the outbreak of World War I.
Appointed Oriental Secretary to the British High Commissioner in Baghdad.
Plays a key role in the Cairo Conference, advocating for the creation of Iraq under King Faisal I.
Establishes the Baghdad Archaeological Museum (later the Iraq Museum).
Dies in Baghdad from an overdose of sleeping pills, believed to be suicide.
Born as Nader Qoli in the Khorasan province of Persia.
Enters the service of the Safavid claimant Tahmasp II and becomes his leading general.
Deposes the last Safavid ruler and is crowned Shah of Persia, founding the Afsharid dynasty.
Invades the Mughal Empire, defeats their army at the Battle of Karnal, and sacks the capital, Delhi.
Conquers Bukhara and Khiva, expanding the Persian empire to its greatest extent in the modern era.
Assassinated by a group of his own officers, leading to the rapid collapse of his empire.
Born in Tekirdağ, Ottoman Empire.
Joins the secret society of the Young Ottomans.
Flees to London and Paris, co-publishes the newspaper Hürriyet (Freedom).
Stages his play Vatan Yahut Silistre (Fatherland or Silistra), leading to his exile in Famagusta, Cyprus.
The first Ottoman Constitution is declared; Kemal serves on the drafting committee.
Sultan Abdülhamid II suspends the constitution and exiles Kemal again.
Dies in exile on the island of Chios.
Born in Tus, Khorasan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Takes refuge with the Nizari Isma'ilis (Assassins), eventually being held in Alamut Castle.
Alamut Castle falls to the Mongols. Tusi enters the service of the Mongol ruler Hulagu Khan.
Construction begins on the Maragheh observatory under Tusi's direction.
Completes his major work on ethics, Akhlaq-i Nasiri (The Nasirean Ethics).
The Maragheh observatory team completes the highly influential astronomical tables, the Zij-i Ilkhani.
Dies in Kadhimiya, near Baghdad, and is buried in a shrine next to the seventh Twelver Shia Imam.
Birth of Nebuchadnezzar II.
Defeats Egyptians at the Battle of Carchemish; ascends to the throne of Babylon.
First siege of Jerusalem. Deports King Jehoiachin and other elites to Babylon.
Second siege of Jerusalem. Destroys the city and Solomon's Temple, beginning the Babylonian Captivity.
Conducts a 13-year siege of the city of Tyre.
Reigns as king, overseeing massive construction projects in Babylon, including the Ishtar Gate.
Dies in Babylon, succeeded by his son Amel-Marduk.
Born Abu Ali Hasan ibn Ali Tusi in Tus, Persia.
Appointed as vizier to the Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan.
Secures the throne for Malik-Shah I after Alp Arslan's assassination, becoming the empire's most powerful figure.
Founds the Nizamiyyah schools across the empire, with the most famous one in Baghdad established in 1065.
Writes the Siyasatnama (Book of Government), a major work on statecraft.
Assassinated near Nahavand, Persia, believed to be by a member of the Order of Assassins.
Birth of Darius.
Seizes the throne of the Achaemenid Empire after overthrowing the usurper Gaumata.
Suppresses widespread revolts across the empire.
Begins work on the Behistun Inscription to legitimize his rule.
Begins construction of the new ceremonial capital, Persepolis.
Launches an unsuccessful campaign against the Scythians in Europe.
The Ionian Revolt begins, marking the start of the Greco-Persian Wars.
The first Persian invasion of Greece ends in defeat at the Battle of Marathon.
Dies in Persia while preparing a second expedition against Greece.
Born in al-Qalamoun, near Tripoli, in Ottoman Syria.
Moved to Cairo, Egypt, to collaborate with Muhammad Abduh.
Founded the influential journal Al-Manar (The Lighthouse).
Following Abduh's death, he became the leading voice of the Islamic Modernist movement.
Founded the 'Society for Preaching and Guidance' in Cairo.
Elected President of the Syrian National Congress.
The abolition of the Ottoman Caliphate deeply impacted his later political thought.
Died in Cairo, Egypt.
Reza Khan is born in Alasht, Mazandaran Province, Persia.
Leads a coup d'état with the Persian Cossack Brigade, becoming Minister of War.
Is voted Shah by the Majlis (parliament), ending the Qajar dynasty and founding the Pahlavi dynasty.
Officially requests foreign nations to use the name 'Iran' instead of 'Persia' in formal correspondence.
Establishes the University of Tehran, a cornerstone of his educational reforms.
Issues the 'Kashf-e hijab' decree, controversially banning all veils in public.
Is forced to abdicate by the Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran during World War II.
Dies in exile in Johannesburg, South Africa.
Born in Mardinu, Babylonia, within the Parthian Empire.
Receives his first revelation from his spiritual twin, the Syzygos.
Receives his second revelation, commanding him to begin his public ministry.
Travels to the Kushan Empire in modern-day Afghanistan and India, encountering Buddhist thought.
Returns to Persia and is granted an audience with the newly crowned Sasanian Emperor, Shapur I, gaining his favor.
Preaches and spreads his religion throughout the Sasanian Empire and beyond under royal protection.
Accused of heresy by the Zoroastrian clergy, he is imprisoned and executed by order of Emperor Bahram I.
Born in Constantinople.
Ascends to the throne after the overthrow of his brother, Mustafa IV.
The 'Auspicious Incident' (Vaka-i Hayriye), where he abolishes the Janissary corps.
The Treaty of Adrianople concludes the Russo-Turkish War, granting autonomy to Serbia and Greece.
Establishes the first official Ottoman newspaper, Takvim-i Vekayi (Calendar of Events).
Dies of tuberculosis, shortly before his planned Edict of Gülhane (part of the Tanzimat) is proclaimed.
Born in Edirne, the Ottoman capital at the time.
First ascends the throne at age 12 after his father's abdication, but is deposed in favor of his father due to court intrigue and external threats.
Ascends the throne for the second time upon the death of his father, Murad II.
Conquers Constantinople after a 53-day siege, effectively ending the Byzantine Empire and making it the new Ottoman capital, Istanbul.
Conquers the Empire of Trebizond, the last major Byzantine successor state.
Wages a long and costly war with the Republic of Venice for control of the Aegean and Adriatic seas.
Compiles the 'Kanunname,' a code of laws that centralized power and shaped the structure of the Ottoman state for centuries.
Dies suddenly during a new military campaign, with the cause of death remaining a subject of historical debate (poison or natural causes).
Born in Salonica (now Thessaloniki, Greece), Ottoman Empire.
Commands Ottoman forces to victory at the Battle of Gallipoli, gaining national fame.
Initiates the Turkish War of Independence against occupying Allied Powers.
Establishes the Grand National Assembly of Turkey in Ankara.
Abolishes the Ottoman Sultanate.
Proclaims the Republic of Turkey and is elected as its first President.
Initiates the replacement of the Arabic-based Ottoman Turkish script with the Latin-based Turkish alphabet.
Grants full universal suffrage to women and is given the surname 'Atatürk' (Father of the Turks) by the parliament.
Dies in Dolmabahçe Palace, Istanbul, at the age of 57.
Born in Kavala, Ottoman Empire (now in Greece).
Arrives in Egypt as second-in-command of an Albanian contingent of the Ottoman army.
Appointed Wāli (governor) of Egypt by the Ottoman Sultan after popular demand.
Consolidates power by massacring the Mamluk leaders at the Citadel in Cairo.
Wages war against the Wahhabis in Arabia on behalf of the Sultan, extending his influence.
Conquers Sudan, seeking gold, slaves, and recruits for his army.
Intervenes in the Greek War of Independence, but his navy is destroyed at the Battle of Navarino.
First Egyptian-Ottoman War. His son Ibrahim Pasha conquers Syria and advances into Anatolia.
Second Egyptian-Ottoman War. European intervention forces him to withdraw from Syria but grants his family hereditary rule of Egypt.
Steps down from power due to failing health.
Dies in Alexandria.
Born in a village in Lower Egypt.
Enrolled at Al-Azhar University in Cairo.
Met his influential mentor, Jamal al-Din al-Afghani.
Exiled from Egypt for his support of the 'Urabi Revolt.
Co-founded the pan-Islamic journal 'Al-Urwah al-Wuthqa' in Paris.
Returned to Egypt from exile.
Appointed Grand Mufti of Egypt, the nation's highest authority on Islamic law.
Died in Alexandria, Egypt.
Born in the village of Ağırnas near Kayseri, Anatolia.
Recruited into the Ottoman Janissary corps through the Devşirme system.
Appointed as the Chief Imperial Architect by Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent.
Completed the Şehzade Mosque in Istanbul, which he considered his first major work.
Completed the Süleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul, a symbol of the Ottoman golden age.
Completed the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne, which he regarded as his masterpiece.
Died in Istanbul at an advanced age and was buried in a tomb of his own design near the Süleymaniye Mosque.
Born in Hamadan, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Leaves Hamadan and travels through the Buyid empire, seeking knowledge and patronage.
Arrives in Nishapur and famously debates the scholar Abu Bakr al-Khwarizmi, earning his title 'Badi' al-Zaman'.
Composes his most famous work, the Maqamat, inventing a new literary genre.
Dies in Herat (in modern-day Afghanistan) at the young age of 38.
Born in Nurs, Bitlis Vilayet, Ottoman Empire.
Travels to Istanbul, proposes educational reforms to combine religious and modern sciences.
Exiled to Burdur, then the remote village of Barla, beginning a 25-year period of exile and imprisonment.
Writes the majority of the Risale-i Nur collection while in exile in Barla.
Faces his first major trial and imprisonment in Eskişehir, along with many of his students.
Dies in Urfa, Turkey. His grave was later moved to an unknown location by the military government.
Born a Kipchak Turk on the Pontic-Caspian Steppe.
Sold as a slave in Syria, eventually purchased by the Ayyubid sultan of Egypt.
Plays a key role in defeating the Seventh Crusade at the Battle of Al Mansurah.
Leads the vanguard of the Mamluk army to a decisive victory over the Mongols at the Battle of Ain Jalut.
Assassinates Sultan Qutuz and ascends to the throne as the Mamluk Sultan.
Captures the Crusader city of Antioch, a major blow to the Crusader states.
Captures the formidable Crusader castles of Krak des Chevaliers and Montfort.
Dies in Damascus, possibly from poison.
Born in Shiraz, Persia.
Leaves Shiraz to study in Baghdad at the Nizamiyya University following the Mongol invasions.
Embarks on a thirty-year journey across the Middle East, North Africa, and Anatolia.
Completes his epic poem 'Bustan' (The Orchard).
Completes his most famous work, 'Gulistan' (The Rose Garden).
Dies in his hometown of Shiraz.
Rises to power, overthrows Ur-Zababa of Kish, and founds the city of Akkad.
Conducts a series of military campaigns, conquering Sumerian city-states like Uruk, Ur, and Lagash.
Establishes the Akkadian Empire, uniting Mesopotamia and creating the world's first known empire.
Dies after a 56-year reign, passing the empire to his son, Rimush.
Born to Ardashir I, the founder of the Sasanian Empire.
Ascends to the throne as the second Sasanian King of Kings.
Defeats the Roman army under Emperor Gordian III at the Battle of Misiche.
Conducts his second campaign against Rome, sacking the major city of Antioch.
Achieves his most famous victory at the Battle of Edessa, defeating and capturing the Roman Emperor Valerian.
Founds the city of Bishapur, using captured Roman soldiers and engineers for its construction.
Dies, leaving behind a greatly expanded and strengthened empire.
Born in Constantinople, son of Sultan Mustafa III.
Ascends to the Ottoman throne during the Austro-Turkish and Russo-Turkish wars.
Signs the Treaty of Jassy, ending the war with Russia and allowing him to focus on domestic reforms.
Formally establishes the 'Nizam-ı Cedid' (New Order) to modernize the state, starting with a new army.
Faces the French invasion of Ottoman Egypt and Syria, led by Napoleon Bonaparte.
Deposed by a Janissary revolt led by Kabakçı Mustafa, and his reforms are abolished.
Assassinated in the Topkapı Palace on the orders of his successor, Mustafa IV, during an attempt to restore him to the throne.
Born in Herat, modern-day Afghanistan.
Ascends the Safavid throne at the age of 16 amidst a military coup against his father.
Signs a harsh peace treaty with the Ottoman Empire, ceding large territories to buy time for internal reforms.
Moves the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan and defeats the Uzbeks near Herat, securing the eastern frontier.
Wages a successful war against the Ottoman Empire, reclaiming territories including Baghdad, Azerbaijan, and Georgia.
With the help of the English navy, captures the strategic island of Hormuz from the Portuguese.
Recaptures Baghdad from the Ottomans.
Dies in Mazandaran, leaving a powerful but unstable legacy due to the lack of a capable heir.
Born in Nineveh, a younger son of King Esarhaddon.
Ascends to the throne of Assyria after the death of his father.
Conducts major campaigns in Egypt, culminating in the sack of Thebes.
A devastating civil war begins with his older brother, Shamash-shum-ukin, ruler of Babylon.
Defeats his brother and sacks the city of Babylon, ending the civil war.
Sacks the Elamite capital of Susa, effectively destroying the rival kingdom of Elam.
Dies, leaving the Assyrian Empire at its peak but severely overstretched and internally weakened.
Born in Cairo, Egypt, to a wealthy family of mixed Turkish, Arab, Circassian, Kurdish, and Greek roots.
Graduated from the School of Law and was sent by Khedive Tewfik to study in France for three years.
Returned to Egypt and was appointed to a prominent position in the court of Khedive Abbas II.
Forced into exile by the British authorities due to his nationalist poetry and support for Egyptian independence. He chose to live in Andalusia, Spain.
Returned to Egypt from exile and was hailed as a national hero.
Crowned 'Amir al-Shu'ara' (The Prince of Poets) by his peers in a grand ceremony, acknowledging his status as the foremost poet in the Arab world.
Died in Cairo at the age of 64.
Born in Lofça (now Lovech, Bulgaria).
Arrived in Constantinople to pursue higher education.
Appointed official historian of the Ottoman Empire.
Appointed head of the Mecelle Commission to codify Ottoman civil law.
Served his first of several terms as Minister of Justice.
Died in Constantinople.
Born in Ray, Persia (modern-day Iran).
Appointed chief physician of the hospital in Ray.
Moved to Baghdad and was appointed director of the main hospital, which he located using an experiment involving meat decomposition.
Wrote 'A Treatise on Smallpox and Measles' (Kitab al-Judari wa al-Hasbah), the first text to clinically differentiate the two diseases.
Died in his hometown of Ray. His students later compiled his notes into the 'Kitab al-Hawi' (The Comprehensive Book).
Born in Baghdad, son of the fifth Abbasid Caliph, Harun al-Rashid.
Harun al-Rashid dies. Al-Ma'mun's half-brother, al-Amin, becomes caliph in Baghdad, while al-Ma'mun governs the eastern province of Khurasan.
The Fourth Fitna, a civil war between al-Amin and al-Ma'mun, culminates in the Siege of Baghdad and al-Ma'mun's victory.
Founds the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) in Baghdad, a major center for translation and research.
Sponsors an expedition of astronomers to measure the Earth's circumference, achieving a remarkably accurate result for the time.
Initiates the 'Mihna' (inquisition) to enforce Mu'tazilite doctrine. Dies during a military campaign against the Byzantine Empire.
Born in Baghdad, Abbasid Caliphate, a major center of learning.
Begins his extensive travels, which would last for over two decades and cover vast regions of Asia and Africa.
Travels through Persia and into the Indus Valley in India, documenting local customs and history.
Completes the first version of his major work, 'Muruj al-Dhahab' (The Meadows of Gold).
Settles in Fustat (Old Cairo), Egypt, where he spends his later years writing and revising his works.
Dies in Fustat, Egypt, leaving behind a monumental but largely lost body of work.
Born in Harran, Upper Mesopotamia (modern-day Turkey).
Moved to Raqqa, Syria, and began his major astronomical observations.
Completed his magnum opus, the Kitāb az-Zīj aṣ-Ṣābi’ (The Sabian Astronomical Tables).
His work was first translated into Latin by Plato of Tivoli as 'De Motu Stellarum' (On the Motion of the Stars).
Died near Samarra, Iraq, while returning from a trip to Baghdad.
Born in Basra (modern-day Iraq).
Moves to Baghdad and begins to gain recognition in the intellectual circles of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Composes his major works, including the 'Book of Animals' (Kitab al-Hayawan) and the 'Book of Eloquence and Exposition' (Kitab al-Bayan wa-al-Tabyin).
Is briefly appointed to an office in the Caliph's court by al-Mutawakkil, but resigns after only three days.
Dies in his hometown of Basra at an advanced age.
Born in Kufa, Abbasid Caliphate, to an aristocratic family of the Kindah tribe.
Flourished in Baghdad under Caliph al-Ma'mun, working at the House of Wisdom to translate and study Greek philosophical and scientific texts.
Continued his scholarly work under the patronage of Caliph al-Mu'tasim.
Fell out of favor during the reign of Caliph al-Mutawakkil. He faced persecution, and his personal library was temporarily confiscated.
His library and favor were restored, allowing him to resume his work.
Died in Baghdad, leaving a legacy of over 260 written works.
Born in Amol, Tabaristan (modern-day Mazandaran Province, Iran).
Left home at age 12 to study in Rayy, then traveled to Baghdad.
Traveled extensively through Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to collect hadith and historical reports.
Settled permanently in Baghdad, where he spent the rest of his life teaching and writing.
Completed his monumental history, 'Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk' (History of the Prophets and Kings).
Faced persecution from the Hanbali school in Baghdad, leading to a form of house arrest.
Died in Baghdad at the age of approximately 83-84.
Born as Muhammad bin Dawud Chaghri.
Ascends to the throne of the Seljuk Empire after the death of his uncle, Tughril.
Captures Ani, the capital of Armenia, expanding Seljuk influence into the Caucasus.
Achieves a decisive victory over the Byzantine Empire at the Battle of Manzikert and captures Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes.
Mortally wounded by a prisoner during a campaign in Transoxiana and dies shortly after, succeeded by his son Malik-Shah I.
